coredata.dc:title: Self-supporting coal liquefaction residue electrode intensifies efficient coupling of hydrogen evolution and carbon dots preparation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Ruzhan
Zhou, Xing
Liu, Quansheng
Zhang, Tong
Fan, Jianming
Zhou, Huacong
Ban, Yanpeng
Li, Na
Gong, Xuzhong
coredata.dc:description: The goal of this work was to solve the problem of mass transfer and carbon conversion in carbon slurry-assisted water electrolysis hydrogen production. Coal liquefaction residue was used as raw material to develop a self-supporting carbon electrode. The one-step method enhances efficient coupling of hydrogen production and carbon dot preparation. Multiple characterizations show the stability and activity of the self-supporting carbon anode. In the anodic carbon oxidation reaction (COR), the oxidation of the carbon source is controllable, which enables the preparation of carbon dots with a yield of 13.66%. At the same time, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is strengthened, and the energy consumption for hydrogen production is 2.23 kWh/Nm3. The efficient coordination between hydrogen production and carbon dot preparation is achieved, and carbon dioxide emissions are also reduced. The use of coal liquefaction residue as a self-supporting carbon electrode provides a sustainable way to realize efficient and clean use of green hydrogen and carbon dots preparation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen
Carbon dots
Self-supporting electrode
Transfer mass
CO2 emissions

Electrochemical analysis of the self-supporting electrode
Structure and characteristic analysis of anode CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and carbon structure of CLR*-12 before and after reaction: (a) schematic diagram of coupling hydrogen production and CDs preparation, (b) SEM of CLR*-12, (c) SEM of CLR*C-12–4, (d) pore size distributions, (e) FTIR, (F) EPR, (g) XPS C1s fitting diagrams, (h) XPS O1s fitting diagrams, (i-j) Raman and ID1 :IG .

Electrochemical performance test results of the self-supporting electrode: (a) LSV of CLR*-T, (b) CLR*-T current density of 1.2–1.8 V vs.RHE, (c) LSV of CLR*-12, (d-e) Tafel and Tafel slope curves, (f-g) EIS curves of 1.80 V vs.RHE and EIS fitting results, (h) characteristic curves of constant potential 1.80 V vs.RHE electrolysis 10 h, and (i) constant potential 1.80 V vs.RHE electrolysis of CLR*-12 and 25 g/L carbon slurry for 10 h.

Hydrogen production performance of CLR*-12: (a) H2 production rate, (b) total charge, (c) total charge of CLR*-12 and 25 g/L carbon slurry, (d) faradaic efficiency, (e) energy consumption, and (f) gas composition.

Structural characterization and optical property analysis of CDs-12–4: (a) yield of CDs, (b) ultraviolet spectra, (c) fluorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, (d) FTIR spectra, (e) Raman spectra, (f) XPS C1s fitting diagram, (g-h) TEM and HRTEM, and (i) XRD.
Comparisons of recent electrolysis performance of water electrolysis and carbon-assisted water electrolysis for hydrogen production in a two-electrode electrolyzer.

row.entry: 0.8 g/L cellulose and lignin-derived carbon slurry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical preparation of CDs using different carbon materials.

head.ce:title: Self-supporting coal liquefaction residue electrode intensifies efficient coupling of hydrogen evolution and carbon dots preparation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ruzhan Bai and Xing Zhou contributed equally to the work and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The goal of this work was to solve the problem of mass transfer and carbon conversion in carbon slurry-assisted water electrolysis hydrogen production. Coal liquefaction residue was used as raw material to develop a self-supporting carbon electrode. The one-step method enhances efficient coupling of hydrogen production and carbon dot preparation. Multiple characterizations show the stability and activity of the self-supporting carbon anode. In the anodic carbon oxidation reaction (COR), the oxidation of the carbon source is controllable, which enables the preparation of carbon dots with a yield of 13.66%. At the same time, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is strengthened, and the energy consumption for hydrogen production is 2.23 kWh/Nm3. The efficient coordination between hydrogen production and carbon dot preparation is achieved, and carbon dioxide emissions are also reduced. The use of coal liquefaction residue as a self-supporting carbon electrode provides a sustainable way to realize efficient and clean use of green hydrogen and carbon dots preparation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water electrolysis is a sustainable and environmentally friendly method to produce high purity hydrogen [1,2]. Due to the four-electron transfer process of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode, the high overpotential and complex reaction mechanism lead to high energy consumption and slow hydrolysis dissociation kinetics, which restricts its large-scale application [3–7]. Therefore, the development of a more favorable and economically efficient alternative to OER (particularly in terms of the anodic oxidation reaction [8,9]), holds great promise. Such an approach has the potential to fundamentally reduce energy requirements for electrocatalytic hydrogen production.

The carbon-assisted electrolysis water (CAEW) hydrogen production strategy utilizes the carbon oxidation reaction at the anode to enhance the hydrogen evolution at the cathode; this approach achieves low-energy hydrogen production by adding carbon particles to the anode electrolyte to form a carbon slurry. The theoretical potential (E0 = 0.21 V) of the complete oxidation reaction of carbon (COR: C + 2H2O − 4e− = CO2 + 4H+) is only one-sixth the size of the theoretical potential of OER (E0 = 1.23 V) [10]. However, the problem of mass transfer by oxidation of carbon particles has been the fundamental factor that limits the efficiency of CAEW for hydrogen production [11,12]. The oxidation reaction of carbon is highly dependent on an electrocatalyst for producing more reactive oxygen species to increase the rate of carbon particle oxidation instead of OER, and the use of precious metal catalysts is the dominant approach [13–15]. Unfortunately, the high cost of these precious metals and the tendency for the electrode surface to become coated with liquid products that result from the oxidation of carbon particles leads to rapid deactivation. In addition, transfer inactivation in the electrolyte after the generation of reactive oxygen species is also a problem that further influences the oxidation rate of carbon particles [16–18].

A promising approach is to construct self-supporting carbon anodes using coke (coal) forming technology by selecting suitable carbon sources. In situ oxidation of self-supporting carbon anodes can be performed after active oxygen is generated on the surface. This serves to prevent the mass transfer of active oxygen within the electrolyte, thereby enhancing the hydrogen evolution rate at the cathode. Coal liquefaction residue (CLR) is a chemically stable substance produced during coal liquefaction [19–21]. CLR is characterized by its high carbon content, consisting primarily of heavy oils, asphaltene, and pre-asphaltenes [22]. The main body of the aromatic structure is similar to the core unit of the raw coal structure model, and it has certain conductivity and defect structure after it is subjected to heat treatment. Because of its high carbon content and condensed aromatic ring structure [25,26], CLR has been recognized as a promising electrode material for the next generation of energy storage devices, such as batteries and super-capacitors [23,24]. Hence, electrical conductivity will be ensured when CLR is utilized as a material for preparing carbon electrodes. CLR is notable for its abundant microcrystalline carbon regions, and it is a good raw material for preparing carbon dots (CDs) via chemical oxidation [27–29]. By controlling the degree of carbon oxidation in self-supporting carbon anodes, the electrolytic preparation of CDs ensures an enhanced hydrogen evolution rate.

Herein, we designed a self-supporting carbon electrode that couples the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and a self-supporting anodic carbon oxidation reaction (COR) to prepare CDs and maximize the use of COR instead of OER to achieve efficient use of carbon sources. This carbon electrode is prepared from CLR and can be used on a large scale. CLR itself has a certain adhesion. After heat treatment, the carbon electrode has strong stability and activity. The results show that the yield of CDs reaches 13.66 % at 1.80 V vs.RHE (initial potential of Pt electrode) and that the energy consumption for hydrogen production is 2.23 kWh/Nm3. The evolution of carbon structure in COR and the structure and properties of CDs were studied using various characterization methods, and the process of controllable oxidation of carbon to prepare CDs was revealed. This work provides a low-carbon and efficient new way to prepare CDs from large-scale coal-based solid waste with low-energy hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolution of carbon structure by a self-supporting electrode
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic diagram of the self-supporting CLR electrode intensified efficient coupling of hydrogen evolution and CDs preparation is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The evolution of carbon structure in COR was studied to determine the process for preparing CDs via oxidation of a CLR carbon electrode (CLR*-12). The morphology of CLR*-12 before and after electrolysis was observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM). SEM images in Fig. 1(b-c) depict the surface morphology of CLR*-12 and CLR*C-12–4. In Fig. 1(b), CLR*-12 exhibits an irregular block and layered structure, and there are voids (Fig. S3). After electrolysis (Fig. 1(c)), the surface of CLR*C-12-4 shows minimal changes.

This is confirmed by the results of the pore size distribution (Fig. 1(d)), in which it can be seen that CLR*-12 is mesoporous. Mesoporous materials have advantages in electron transport because the presence of mesopores can increase the contact area between an electrolyte and electrode, which is beneficial for the electrode reaction [30]. The difference is that the peak intensity of CLR*C-12–4 is weaker. The carbon activity is reduced for long-time electrolysis, and the carbon structure pore collapses, resulting in a weaker pore size.

Additionally, the pyrolysis behavior of CLR*-12 and CLR*C-12-4 in a N2 atmosphere was studied, and the difference in the carbon structure before and after electrolysis was judged. The pyrolysis process of CLR*-12 and CLR*C-12-4 is divided into three stages (Fig. S4). Before 350°C, the thermal weight loss is almost unchanged; from 350 °C to 500 °C, the weight loss rate is the fastest, and at 450 °C, the weight loss rate reaches a maximum that is due to the dealkylation of the aliphatic chain of the aromatic structure and the dehydrogenation of the cycloalkyl group [31]. After 500 °C, polycondensation is the main reaction. The weight loss rate of CLR*C-12-4 in the second stage was 9.36 %, which was higher than that of CLR*-12; the weight loss rate of CLR*C-12-4 at 1000 °C was 15.41 %. This shows that the structure of CLR changes during the electrochemical oxidation process, and the thermal stability decreases.

To study how the carbon structure of CLR*-12 changed after electrooxidation, the carbon crystal forms of the samples before and after electrolysis were evaluated using X-ray diffraction. As seen from the XRD results, CLR*-12 and CLR*C-12–4 have a wide peak around 25.80° (Fig. S5); this corresponds to C (002) and is relative to commercial graphite carbon (26.48°) [32]. The difference is that the C (002) peak intensity of CLR*C-12–4 is weaker than that of CLR*-12. The aromaticity (fa) decreases, and the stacking height (Lc) of the aromatic lamellae decreases (Table S1). Part of the FTIR spectra of CLR*-12 and CLR*C-12–4 are shown in Fig. 1(e), and the full FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. S6. A broad band centered at 3435 cm−1 indicates O-H stretching vibrations typical of hydroxyl groups. The wide band centered at 1630 cm−1 relates to the C = O stretching vibration and is commonly seen in carboxyl or aldehyde groups. After electrooxidation, the peak intensity of CLR*C-12–4 was enhanced.

Meanwhile, according to the EPR spectral results (Fig. 1(f)), the characteristic peak signal of PFRs in CLR*C-12–4 is enhanced compared to that of the PFRs in CLR*-12. The reason for this is that most of the oxygen-rich groups in the carbon skeleton are exfoliated during the continuous oxidation process, and PFRs are produced [33]. PFRs can also adsorb hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [34]. To further confirm that hydroxyl radicals are the active components of carbon oxidation, Fig. S7 shows the yield of CDs before and after the quenching of hydroxyl radicals. When 1 M and 2 M tert-butyl alcohol were added to the electrolyte, the yield of CDs respectively decreased to 9.9 % and 8.1 % after 40 h electrolysis, and this indicates that ·OH was generated during the electrolysis.

Furthermore, in the C1s fitting results, the C1s spectrum can be divided into five peaks: C-C bonds (sp2) at 284.8 eV, defects (sp3) at 285.5 eV, C-O bonds at 286.5 eV, C = O bonds at 287.5 eV, and O-C = O bonds at 288.5 eV (Fig. 1(g)) [35]. The C1s fitting results show a continuous increase in the relative oxygen content in carbon throughout the electrochemical process; specifically, it increases from 13.12 % to 71.52 % (Fig. S8). This indicates that there is ongoing oxidation throughout the process. Notably, the relative content of C-O bonds initially increases and then decreases. CLR*C-12–2 exhibits the highest relative content at 31.49 %; this suggests that before 20 h of electrolysis, the main bonds between carbon and oxygen were C-O bonds, and some of these were oxidized to C = O and O-C = O bonds. When the reaction time was further prolonged, CLR*C-12–3 displayed a lower oxygen content than CLR*C-12–2; it also showed an increase in the relative content of C-C bonds and defects and a decrease in the relative content of C-O bonds. The fitting results of O1s are shown in Fig. 1(h) and Fig. S9). It was confirmed that the increase of O after electrooxidation of CLR*-12 was mainly in the form of C = O (531.3 eV), O = C-O (533.8 eV), and adsorbed oxygen (535.80 eV) [36].

Next, Raman spectroscopy was used to observe the structural changes of the CLR*-12 carbon anode during electrooxidation. Notably, this spectrum features two distinct peaks at 1350 cm−1 (D1 band) and 1580 cm−1 (G band) [37]. The D1 band is indicative of the stretching vibrations of C-C and C = C bonds and signifies the presence of disorder and defects within the carbon crystal lattice (Fig. 1(i)). On the other hand, the G band represents the sp2 hybridization and π-electron stretching vibrations that are characteristic of graphene or monolayer graphite structures. The graphitization degree and defect sites within the CDs are assessed by calculating the ID1/IG ratio. Fig. 1(j) and Table S2 reveal that the ID1/IG ratio gradually increased and then sharply decreased. These two trends alternated as carbon underwent changes. For instance, the ID1/IG ratio initially increased from an initial value of 1.29 to 1.41 (at 20 h), and then it abruptly dropped to 1.31 (at 30 h). By combining the XPS C1s spectra, the carbon structure during the electrooxidation process in two stages can be summarized as follows: First, the carbon surface of CLR*-T was adsorbed with ·OH to form C-OH, and this was subsequently oxidized into higher oxygen-containing functional groups. Second, carbon structures that were rich in oxygen peeled off into the liquid phase, exposing more defective carbon. Continued adsorption of ·OH led to the formation of C-OH, and this resulted in the alternating oxidation of carbon throughout the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of the self-supporting electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The above structure indicates that the carbon oxidation reaction is alternately controllable during the electro-oxidation of carbon. However, water oxidation reactions (WORs) and COR may both occur at CLR*-T. As seen from the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) results, LSV profiles of CLR*-T oxidation have a lower onset potential (0.33 − 0.42 V vs.RHE) than that of OER (1.80 V vs.RHE) in the same electrolyte; the exception is CLR*-6 (Fig. 2 (a)). This indicates the excellent electrochemical properties of CLR*-T. All of the CLR*-T samples exhibit two oxidation stages, COR I and CORII; the exception is CLR*-6, which has a low oxidation activity because of its lower electrical conductivity. COR I is typically oxidation of disordered domains and molecularly functionalized carbon structures [38,39]. However, these carbon structures have intrinsically low conductivity, and this prevents higher currents from being achieved for COR I. COR II (>1.20 V vs.RHE) involves a catalytic oxidation stage that is related to water. In the range of (1.20 to 1.80 V vs.RHE) (Fig. 2(b)), both CLR*-12 and CLR*-14 exhibit higher current densities than CLR*-9 with CLR*-12 and CLR*-14 showing similar current densities. As seen from other electrochemical tests (Fig. S10), the electrochemical performance of CLR*-14 is slightly better than that of CLR*-12. This is because of the decreased oxygen content and the fat side chain of the self-supporting electrode and the increased graphitization degree with an increase in carbonization temperature (Fig. S11). However, considering the carbonization temperature, the relative optimal conditions of CLR*-12.

Graphite is a good conductive agent that is not a noble metal, and it plays an important role in electrochemistry. Fig. 2(c), CLR*-12 exhibits an excellent current density relative to that of graphite. The Tafel polarization curve of CLR*-12 is shown in Fig. 2(d). The corresponding equilibrium potentials of G and CLR*-12 are (0.463 V vs.RHE) and (0.534 V vs.RHE), respectively. It is clear that the stability of the carbon electrode is strong. The Tafel slope is closely related to the electron transfer rate; when the Tafel slope is smaller, the electron transfer rate is faster, and the electrochemical reaction kinetics are better. As shown in Fig. 2(e), CLR*-12 has a lower slope relative to graphite and Pt. Then, the anodic reaction rate was further studied using EIS. As shown in Fig. 2(f-g), when the anode potential was (1.80 V vs.RHE), the Rct of CLR*-12 was the smallest; specifically, the value was 2.04 Ω, indicating that CLR*-12 has a faster electrochemical reaction rate.

Fig. 2(h) shows the constant potential electrolysis characteristic curve of CLR*-12. From the anode potential of 1.80 (V vs.RHE) electrolysis for 10 h, it can be seen that the current of CLR*-12 is stable and that it has a long lifetime. It was also observed that the current densities of CLR*-12 were higher than those of G. Also, by comparing CLR*-12 to its carbon paste with a concentration of 25 g/L, it can be seen that the self-supporting carbon electrode (CLR*-12) has a superior current density (Fig. 2(i), Table S3). These electrochemical test results emphasize the superior electrochemical performance of CLR*-12, indicating that they are promising candidates for the production of hydrogen evolution and CDs with enhanced properties.

The above electrochemical experiments show that CLR*-12 has strong stability and activity. To confirm that CLR*-12 can enhance HER, the hydrogen production effect and the energy consumption of the CLR*-12 anode were studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) shows the results of the hydrogen production rate of CLR*-12. The initial potential of the Pt anode was 1.80 V vs.RHE, and thus, the hydrogen production rate of the Pt anode was 0 mL·h−1·cm−2. The hydrogen production rates of the G and CLR*-12 anodes are 4.12 mL·h−1·cm−2 and 11.56 mL·h−1·cm−2, respectively. Combined with the hydrogen production rate of CLR*-T (Fig. S12(a)), it is clear that the CLR*-12 anode effectively enhances the hydrogen production of the cathode. In the anode reaction, the total charge accumulation is proportional to the hydrogen generation rate, and this indirectly reflects the hydrogen production of the cathode. Fig. S12(b) shows the total charge accumulation of CLR*-T anodes during hydrogen production. The values of the total charge accumulation of the CLR*-9, CLR*-12, and CLR*-14 anodes are 3.24C, 10.19C, and 11.35C, respectively. From comparing CLR*-12 with G and its carbon slurry at a concentration of 25 g/L, it is clear that the self-supporting carbon electrode obtains more charge during the potentiostatic electrolysis process (Fig. 3(b-c)).

The calculated Faraday efficiency and hydrogen production energy consumption are shown in Fig. 3(d-e). The Faraday efficiency of CLR*-12 is 91.13 %, which is 1.21 times greater than that of G. The hydrogen production energy consumption is 2.23 kWh/Nm3. We compared the energy consumption of this experiment to that of other water electrolysis or carbon-assisted water electrolysis results. As shown in Table 1 , for hydrogen production, the energy consumption of CLR*-T is in the range of 2.15 − 2.58 kWh/Nm3 (Fig. S12), and this indicates that the CLR*-T anode consumes less energy for hydrogen production.

The potential gas products of CLR*-12 were studied during COR. Fig. 3(f) shows the composition and proportions of gases in 5 h of CLR*-12 anode electrolysis. As seen from the figure, the gases produced by the CLR*-12 anode are O2, CO2, and CO. CO2 accounts for 4.57 %. Compared to G, the oxygen-hydrogen ratio of the CLR*-12 electrode is 0.33. This shows that the CLR*-12 anode performs well in hydrogen production and has high hydrogen production performance. A very interesting observation was made; specifically, the electrolyte solution slowly turned dark yellow. However, this was not observed when graphite was used as the anode (Fig. S13). This explains why the carbon oxidation reaction of the CLR carbon electrode produces a low concentration of CO2, that is, a substance with colloidal properties might be produced (Fig. S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and characteristic analysis of anode CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and properties of the abovementioned colloidal properties were analyzed using various material characterization methods, and it was proved that the substance studied is CDs. Also, CLR*-12 is a relatively optimal condition for preparing CDs (Fig. S15, Table S4, and Table S5). At this point, the structure and yield of CDs are poor and need to be further optimized. To facilitate the description, the CDs that were prepared after n hours of CLR*-12 reaction are denoted as CDs-12-n (where n = 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5). The yields of CDs that were obtained using various reaction times are shown in Fig. 4 (a). It is observed that with prolonged reaction times, the yield of CDs initially increases and then decreases. The highest yield (13.66 %) was achieved by CDs-12–4, and this trend can be attributed to the reaction reaching an equilibrium point after around 40 h. Further prolonging the reaction time may lead to reduced reactivity of the reactants, aggregation of CDs, or partial structural damage. Notably, CDs derived from high-rank coal exhibited a deep red color, which is comparable to the color of the CDs that were synthesized in this study after 40 h of oxidation (Fig. S16), in which we achieved a production rate ranging from 10.83 % to 16.30 %. Furthermore, Table 2 provides a comparison of CDs syntheses using electrochemical methods with various materials, and this highlights the superiority of electrochemically synthesized CDs from CLR.

The structure and properties of CDs were studied to further explain why electrolysis of CLR*-12 for 40 h is better. The optical characteristics of CDl-12–4 were assessed using ultraviolet and fluorescence spectroscopy. The ultraviolet spectrum (Fig. 4(b)) reveals that the absorbance profile of CDl-12–4 exhibits pronounced absorption across the entire ultraviolet range. This absorption intensified dramatically as the wavelength decreased, reaching a peak at 230 nm. The absorbance values at 350 nm and 230 nm correspond to the n-π* structure of the luminescent groups and the π-π* structure of sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [47,48]. These findings suggest that the structure of the CDs comprises unsaturated carbons with heteroatoms and graphitized carbon elements; this affirms the presence of oxygen-containing structures and crystalline structures within the CDs. Upon comparing the absorbance of the CDl-12-n (where n = 1, 2, 3, 5) systems (Fig. S17(a)), it is evident that CDl-12–4 exhibits stronger absorbance throughout the ultraviolet spectrum; specifically, the values at 350 nm and 230 nm are 0.329 and 4.447, respectively. This implies that there is a higher concentration of oxygen-containing structures and crystalline structures in CDl-12–4. Fig. 4(c) shows the fluorescence spectrum of CDl-12–4 at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm; overall the spectrum first increases and then decreases with an increase in wavelength, reaching a peak at 455 nm. Compared with CDl-12-n (where n = 1, 2, 3, and 5) (Fig. S17(b)), CDl-12–4 has the strongest fluorescence intensity, and this is consistent with the ultraviolet spectrum results. The reason why the fluorescence intensity of CDl-12–5 is lower than that of CDl-12–4 may be that the CDs are agglomerated or that the partial structure of the CDs is destroyed, and thus, the fluorescence is quenched. The inset photo shows that when CDl-12–4 is under 350 nm ultraviolet light, it emits a yellow-green fluorescence. The relative quantum yield (φ) of CDl-12–4 was calculated using a comparative method, and a φ value of 1.28 % was found (Table S5).

The molecular structure, chemical bonds, and varieties of functional groups in the CDs-12–4 were meticulously analyzed using FTIR, Raman, and XPS techniques. The FTIR spectrum of CDs-12–4 (Fig. 4(d)) shows several distinct features. A broad band centered at 3435 cm−1 corresponds to O-H stretching vibrations that are typical of hydroxyl groups. The wide band centered at 1630 cm−1 corresponds to the C = O stretching vibration and is commonly seen in carboxyl or aldehyde groups. The broad band centered at 1580 cm−1 is related to the C = C stretching vibration of the aromatic skeleton. The peak at 1430 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O-H stretching vibration of phenolic hydroxyl groups. Also, the peak at 1110 cm−1 corresponds to C-O stretching vibrations in ethers or alcohols [49]. Compared to FTIR data for CDs-12-n (where n = 1, 2, 3, and 5) (Fig. S17(c)), the peak intensities of the oxygen-containing group and the benzene ring carbon structure of CDs-12–4 are relatively strongest. This indicates that the CDs (CDs-12–4) that were prepared via 4 h of reaction are rich in polyoxygenic groups and that the graphite carbon crystallites also increase. This enhancement may be attributed to the further oxidation of substances that contain C-O bonds (such as alcohols and phenols) into compounds that have C = O bonds (such as ketones, aldehydes, and carboxylic acids) [49] during the electrolysis process of CLR*-12. As observed from the Raman spectra (Fig. 4(e), Fig. S17(d)) of CDs-12-n, CDs-12–4 had the most graphitized carbon crystallites (ID1:IG = 1.03). This also confirms that 40 h of oxidation is deep so that some graphite carbon crystallites are oxidized and etched from the CLR carbon.

XPS results further validate the rich content of oxygen-containing functional groups within the CDs structures. Fig. 4(f) shows the C1s fitting diagram of CDs-12–4. As seen, the oxygen-containing groups of CDs-12–4 are C-O and O-C = O. Also, as seen from Fig. S15(e), the oxygen-containing groups of CDs are uniform with longer oxidation time. The oxygen-containing groups in CDs-12-n (n > 1) are C-O and O-C = O. Compared to the C1s fitting results of CLR*-12, it is found that there was higher content of oxygen-containing groups in CDs-12–4 (29.12 %) (Fig. S10(f)) than in CLR*-12 (13.12 %) (Fig. S8(b)). From the TG experiment of CDs, it is determined that the mass loss at 281 °C is a result of the pyrolysis of unstable oxygen-containing functional groups (such as COOH), and this indicates that the CDs contain a large amount of –COOH (Fig. S18).

The microstructures of CDs were further investigated using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(g-h) for CDs-12–4. These images reveal that CDs-12–4 features well-distributed CDs with an average size of 2.18 nm and an interplanar spacing of 0.36 nm. As seen from the XRD results, CDs-12–4 has a wide peak around 26.76° (Fig. 4(i)), corresponding to C (002). This microstructure is consistent with what has been reported in the literature [49], and it indicates a high level of crystallinity in CDs-12–4. In contrast, TEM images of CDs-12–5 (Fig. S19) demonstrate that these CDs are less uniformly distributed, and this confirms that extending the reaction time to 50 h disrupts some CDs structures, leading to a decreased yield. The liquid products of CLR*-12 after electrooxidation were also identified by GC–MS, and the results are shown in Fig. S20. The liquid phase product is mainly cyclohexanone, which can be used as raw materials in the chemical industry, medicine, and the food industry.

A one-step strategy was successfully studied for the efficient coupling of hydrogen evolution and CDs preparation using self-supporting CLR electrode. CLR*-12 exhibited remarkable stability and activity. The carbon anode can work for more than 50 h at a current of 1.80 V vs.RHE (initial potential of Pt electrode). In addition, the carbon oxidation of CLR*-12 is controllable, and thus, it can produce an additional product of high-value CDs and reduce the production of CO2. The yield of CDs reached 13.66 % at 1.80 V, and CO2 accounted for 4.57 % of the gas composition. At the same time, HER was stronger, and the energy consumption for hydrogen production was 2.23 kWh/Nm3. The integrated approach maximizes the benefits of carbon-assisted electrolysis of water. This work provides a theoretical foundation for using coal-derived materials as carbon sources in electrochemical CDs production coupled with hydrogen generation.

Ruzhan Bai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xing Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Quansheng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Conceptualization. Tong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianming Fan: Software, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Huacong Zhou: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yanpeng Ban: Software, Investigation. Na Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xuzhong Gong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
